# DOSSIER: OPPORTUNITY MARKETING

> **Version:** 1.0.0
> **Created:** 2026-03-15
> **Last Updated:** 2026-03-15
> **Primary Source:** Dan Kennedy — Opportunity Marketing Concepts (OMC)
> **Contributing Sources:** Dan Kennedy (CC, 7FA, 12BBS, ACC, MM), Alex Hormozi, Russell Brunson
> **DNA Elements:** 391 (from OMC) + cross-references from 6 other DK sources
> **Guest Speakers:** Jeff Walker, Craig Simpson, George Douglas

---

## DEFINITION

Opportunity Marketing is the strategic repositioning of any offer from the **repair** or **improvement** spectrum to the **opportunity** spectrum. This is not a copy technique — it is a fundamental reframe of how the prospect perceives what is being sold. ^[FIL-DK-319]

The core thesis: **only opportunity is linked to DESIRE.** Need is boring (toilet paper). Obligation is unmotivating (helping a friend move). Opportunity is exciting (lottery, new identity, becoming something new). ^[FIL-DK-320, FIL-DK-323, FIL-DK-324]

The practical implication: **repositioning any offer from repair/improvement to opportunity multiplies conversion AND liberates price elasticity** — because opportunity decouples price from income ratios. People don't comparison-shop opportunities the way they comparison-shop repairs. ^[FIL-DK-386, FIL-DK-403]

---

## THE REPAIR → IMPROVEMENT → OPPORTUNITY SPECTRUM

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                             │
│  REPAIR              IMPROVEMENT            OPPORTUNITY                     │
│  ─────              ───────────            ───────────                     │
│  Linked to NEED     Linked to OBLIGATION   Linked to DESIRE                │
│  Boring (toilet     Not exciting           Exciting (lottery,              │
│  paper level)       (gym membership)       new identity)                   │
│                                                                             │
│  "Fix what broke"   "Make it better"       "Become something new"          │
│                                                                             │
│  Lowest price       Mid price              Highest price elasticity        │
│  elasticity         elasticity             (income-to-price decoupled)     │
│                                                                             │
│  Comparison         Some comparison        No comparison shopping          │
│  shopping           shopping               (unique = incomparable)         │
│                                                                             │
│  ← MORE COMPETITIVE                    LESS COMPETITIVE →                  │
│  ← LOWER MARGINS                       HIGHER MARGINS →                    │
│  ← RATIONAL DECISION                   EMOTIONAL DECISION →               │
│                                                                             │
│  ^[FIL-DK-319]      ^[FIL-DK-323]          ^[FIL-DK-324, FIL-DK-386]      │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Repositioning Examples

| Before (Repair/Improvement) | After (Opportunity) | Mechanism |
|----------------------------|---------------------|-----------|
| "Fix your sales" | "Build a sales machine that runs without you" | Identity + system |
| "Lose weight" | "Join a transformation community becoming new versions of themselves" | Community + identity |
| "Get more leads" | "Discover the magnetic system that makes leads come to YOU" | Inversion + tangibility |
| "Improve your coaching" | "Become a $50K/day advisor (Dan Kennedy model)" | Aspirational identity |
| "Financial planning" | "The wealth blueprint that 1% use and 99% never learn" | Exclusivity + opportunity |

---

## CORE PSYCHOLOGICAL MODELS

### 1. The 11 Agreements for Attainability ^[MM-DK-280]

Before buying an opportunity, the prospect must sequentially agree on 11 points. Each agreement is a hurdle — failure at any point stops the sale:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  SEQUENTIAL AGREEMENT CHAIN:                                                │
│                                                                             │
│  1. General need exists (market is real)                                    │
│  2. Specific approach could work (methodology is valid)                     │
│  3. This particular opportunity is credible                                 │
│  4. The person presenting it is credible                                    │
│  5. It works for people "like me"                                           │
│  6. It could work specifically for ME                                       │
│  7. I could actually DO this                                                │
│  8. The timing is right NOW                                                 │
│  9. The price is appropriate                                                │
│  10. The risk is manageable                                                 │
│  11. This is the BEST use of my resources right now                         │
│                                                                             │
│  FAILURE AT ANY STEP = NO SALE                                              │
│  Marketing must engineer each agreement in sequence.                        │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 2. The Conscious Mind as Belief Fortress ^[MM-DK-270, FIL-DK-326]

The conscious mind is not a rational decision-maker — it is a **survival fortress** whose only purpose is to protect existing beliefs. Every sales pitch is perceived as a THREAT to this fortress.

Implications:
- Direct contradiction of existing beliefs triggers **defense mode** (stronger resistance)
- Opportunity bypasses the fortress by showing something **fundamentally different** from what was already rejected
- The fortress defends against FAMILIAR threats — novel opportunity formats slip through
- This is why the Lucy/Charlie Brown model works: different presentation = different category = no fortress response

### 3. The Gullibility-Skepticism Axis ^[MM-DK-272, FIL-DK-329]

The goal is to move prospects from **skepticism** (defensive, calculating, resistant) to **gullibility** — a state where desire and vulnerabilities override rational calculation.

```
SKEPTICISM ◄──────────────────────────────► GULLIBILITY

  Rational          Emotional override
  Calculating       Desire-driven
  Defensive         Receptive
  Comparison        "I want THIS"
  shopping

  NEW opportunity is easier to believe
  than repair of familiar failure.
```

Key insight: Gullibility is NOT stupidity. "Hype works on intelligent, educated, sophisticated consumers." ^[FIL-DK-345] The mechanism is desire, not ignorance.

### 4. The Lucy/Charlie Brown Model ^[MM-DK-281, FIL-DK-339]

Prospects are Charlie Browns — they have already run to kick the football and landed on their backs. Multiple times. With different Lucys.

**Rule:** Do not show them Lucy and a football. Show them something that LOOKS fundamentally different. Same underlying mechanism, different packaging. The fortress defends against pattern recognition — break the pattern.

### 5. The Toaster Principle ^[MM-DK-282, FIL-DK-372]

Present your opportunity like a **toaster** — a tangible, named appliance where you push a button and toast comes up.

**Rules:**
- Give it a NAME (a "thing" sounds like a kitchen appliance)
- Make it TANGIBLE (not a process, not a journey, not "personal growth")
- Push button → toast comes up (simple input → clear output)
- Do NOT describe the process of making bread

"A thing has to sound like a kitchen appliance, not like a big elaborate process." ^[FIL-DK-372]

### 6. The Dissatisfaction Gap ^[MM-DK-319]

Spend **70%+ of the presentation building the gap** between current reality and desired state BEFORE showing the solution. The bigger the gap, the more the prospect NEEDS the bridge — and the less price matters.

---

## THREE DRAWERS TOOLBOX ^[MM-DK-297]

When something isn't working, the answer is in one of three drawers:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                                             │
│  DRAWER 1: PRODUCT/MESSAGE                                                  │
│  What are you selling and how are you communicating it?                     │
│  → Is the offer positioned as opportunity, not repair?                     │
│  → Does it have a name? Does it sound like a toaster?                      │
│  → Is the copy entering the conversation in their mind?                    │
│                                                                             │
│  DRAWER 2: PROCESS                                                          │
│  How are you making the sale?                                               │
│  → Multi-step or one-shot?                                                 │
│  → Sideways sales letter (PLF) or frontal assault?                         │
│  → Are you using delayed sale where appropriate?                           │
│                                                                             │
│  DRAWER 3: PROSPECTS                                                        │
│  Are you investing in the BEST prospects?                                   │
│  → Response lists vs compiled lists?                                       │
│  → Top 20% (5-15-80 Rule)?                                                 │
│  → "Screw them. Leave them in the bottom of the pyramid." ^[FIL-DK-367]   │
│                                                                             │
│  RULE: 80% of problems are in Drawer 3.                                    │
│  Most marketers obsess over Drawers 1-2 and ignore 3.                      │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## GUEST SPEAKER CONTRIBUTIONS

### Jeff Walker — Product Launch Formula ^[FIL-DK-392, MM-DK-325]

**Core concept:** The sideways sales letter. Instead of one 50-page sales letter, tell the same story over 10-12 days with 3-4 pieces of prelaunch content.

**8 Mental Triggers:** Authority, Reciprocity, Community, Social Proof, Anticipation, Scarcity, Likeability, Events/Rituals.

**Key insights:**
- Prelaunch content creates viral word of mouth (audience does the selling) ^[FIL-DK-396]
- "People don't want to learn how to fish" — done-for-you beats DIY ^[FIL-DK-402]
- Long-term thinking as competitive advantage ^[FIL-DK-394]
- Delayed sale can be BETTER sale — less remorse, more conversion ^[FIL-DK-391, FIL-DK-405]

**Connection to Opportunity Marketing:** PLF is the PROCESS (Drawer 2) for selling opportunity. It builds the dissatisfaction gap over days, engineers all 11 Agreements sequentially, and creates community-based social proof.

---

### Craig Simpson — Direct Mail Science ^[FIL-DK-388, MM-DK-307]

**Core concept:** List selection science + LTV liberation.

**Key insights:**
- Ken Roberts Company: 11 products in 17 weeks + quarterly reactivation ^[FIL-DK-388]
- Compiled vs Response lists — response lists 40x more productive ^[MM-DK-307]
- Format rotation: postcard then envelope to same list = virtually no duplicate response ^[MM-DK-309]
- LTV liberation: higher LTV = larger addressable market = can spend more to acquire ^[MM-DK-320]
- Market capacity wall: honeymoon → gradual creep → wall. Change economics, not creative. ^[MM-DK-302]

**Connection to Opportunity Marketing:** Craig demonstrates that when creative has peaked (Drawer 1 optimized), the answer is in Drawer 3 (better prospects via list science) and backend economics (raise LTV to expand addressable market).

---

### George Douglas — Trust-Based Distance Selling ^[FIL-DK-359, HEUR-DK-423]

**Core concept:** Sell $10K products at distance through TRUST, not pressure.

**Key data points:**
- $52M in career, $10K average sale, never met most clients ^[HEUR-DK-423]
- Used 3-hour cassette tapes — prospects listened 5x before buying ^[HEUR-DK-424]
- 42 programs available with $50 swap fee (cafeteria model) ^[HEUR-DK-428, HEUR-DK-429]
- Bonus system for staff: self-managed, self-policed ^[MM-DK-314]
- "Dad, they trusted you" — sincerity as sales mechanism ^[FIL-DK-375]
- "Never worked a day in my life — everything was wonderful" ^[FIL-DK-359]

**Connection to Opportunity Marketing:** George proves that opportunity positioning + trust makes high-ticket distance selling viable. Length of content doesn't matter if the content is what they're looking for. The longer they engage, the more trust builds, the higher the ticket.

| Franchise conversion funnel | 1,400 → 300 qualified → 1-2 buy | Expected ratios for high-ticket | HEUR-DK-400 |
| VA prescription non-fill | 80% don't fill FREE prescriptions | Proving: price ≠ barrier, desire is | HEUR-DK-398 |
| Glenn Turner scale | 500K+ people × $5K in 3.5 years | Opportunity vehicle at massive scale | HEUR-DK-420 |
| Carlton Sheets record | 14 years continuous infomercial | Same creative, same offer, opportunity | HEUR-DK-430 |
| ICS ad longevity | Same ad from 1919 to 2010+ | When opportunity is real, creative lasts | HEUR-DK-432 |
| Continuity advantage | 1 charge/month vs 28-31 days trying to quit | Asymmetry of subscription economics | HEUR-DK-408 |
| Compiled vs Response lists | Response lists 40x more productive | Drawer 3 (prospects) beats Drawer 1 (message) | MM-DK-307 |
| Format rotation | Postcard + envelope = virtually no dupe | Same list, different format = fresh audience | MM-DK-309 |
| Dissatisfaction gap | 70%+ of presentation before solution | Build gap, then bridge it | MM-DK-319 |

## CROSS-REFERENCES WITH OTHER MEGA BRAIN THEMES

| Theme | Connection | Overlap |
|-------|-----------|---------|
| **Offers & Pricing** (Hormozi) | Hormozi's $100M Offers framework is opportunity positioning applied to offer structure. His "dream outcome / perceived likelihood / time delay / effort" equation maps to DK's 11 Agreements. | HIGH |
| **Sales Psychology** (Cole Gordon) | Cole's objection handling addresses individual hurdles in the 11 Agreements chain. His qualification framework = DK's Drawer 3 (invest in best prospects). | MEDIUM |
| **Funnel Architecture** (Russell Brunson) | RB's funnel types are delivery mechanisms for opportunity positioning. His "attractive character" framework = DK's personality-driven marketing applied to digital. | HIGH |
| **Direct Response Copy** (DK-CC, DK-MM) | OMC provides the strategic WHY behind copy decisions. CC/MM provide the tactical HOW. Combined: position as opportunity (OMC) then write the copy (CC/MM). | VERY HIGH |
| **Consulting & Coaching** (DK-ACC) | ACC's ascension ladder is an opportunity structure. Fee presentation ("quote and shut up") works because opportunity liberates price from income ratios. | HIGH |
| **Recession Proofing** (DK-12BBS) | 12BBS's "Three Recession Responses" = Drawer 1 (sell differently), Drawer 3 (sell to different people), or create new opportunity. OMC provides the framework for the third option. | MEDIUM |

---

## APPLICATION FRAMEWORK: REPOSITIONING ANY OFFER

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  STEP 1: IDENTIFY CURRENT POSITION ON SPECTRUM                              │
│  └── Is your offer positioned as repair, improvement, or opportunity?      │
│  └── Test: Would a prospect be EXCITED or OBLIGATED to buy?               │
│                                                                             │
│  STEP 2: REFRAME AS OPPORTUNITY                                             │
│  └── What NEW IDENTITY does the buyer become?                              │
│  └── What EXCLUSIVE SYSTEM makes it feel different from past failures?     │
│  └── Apply Toaster Principle: give it a NAME, make it tangible             │
│                                                                             │
│  STEP 3: ENGINEER THE 11 AGREEMENTS                                        │
│  └── Map your marketing sequence to the 11 hurdles                         │
│  └── Ensure each step earns the next agreement                             │
│  └── Use PLF (sideways sales letter) as delivery mechanism                 │
│                                                                             │
│  STEP 4: BUILD THE DISSATISFACTION GAP                                     │
│  └── 70%+ of presentation before showing solution                          │
│  └── Before > After (before is more important)                             │
│  └── Use story devices (Blackie story, mystery opportunity)                │
│                                                                             │
│  STEP 5: BYPASS THE FORTRESS                                                │
│  └── Does it LOOK different from what they already rejected?               │
│  └── Lucy/Charlie Brown test: would they recognize the pattern?            │
│  └── Novel format + novel name + novel mechanism = fortress bypass          │
│                                                                             │
│  STEP 6: OPTIMIZE ECONOMICS (when creative peaks)                          │
│  └── Don't change the ad — change the economics                           │
│  └── Raise LTV to expand addressable market                                │
│  └── Response lists > compiled lists (40x ROI difference)                  │
│  └── Backend products extend the math                                      │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## THE IMMUTABLE PYRAMID ^[MM-DK-299, FIL-DK-364]

```
                    ╱╲
                   ╱  ╲
                  ╱ 1% ╲         ← RICH
                 ╱──────╲
                ╱  4%    ╲       ← FINANCIALLY SECURE
               ╱──────────╲
              ╱            ╲
             ╱    54%       ╲    ← STILL WORKING AT 65
            ╱────────────────╲
           ╱     5%           ╲  ← BROKE
          ╱────────────────────╲
         ╱       36%            ╲← DEAD
        ╱────────────────────────╲

  These numbers have NOT changed since 1954.
  Business model must reflect this reality.
  "The money is in marketing to the most likely buyer.
   Screw them. Leave them in the bottom of the pyramid."
```
